Topological Sorting - Complete Guide

Overview

Topological sorting is a linear ordering of vertices in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) such that for every directed edge (u, v), vertex u comes before v in the ordering.

Key Characteristics

  • DAG Only: Works only on Directed Acyclic Graphs
  • Multiple Valid Orders: Many valid topological orders may exist
  • Dependency Resolution: Solves problems with prerequisite/dependency relationships
  • Applications: Task scheduling, build systems, course planning, dependency resolution

Complexity Analysis

Approach Time Complexity Space Complexity Use Case
DFS (Kahn’s Algorithm) O(V + E) O(V) General purpose, cycle detection
BFS (In-degree) O(V + E) O(V) Finding all orderings, level-by-level
Tree Centroid Finding O(V + E) O(V) Undirected trees, find center/minimize height
All Topological Sorts O(V! × (V + E)) O(V) Small graphs, all permutations

References

  • techbridge : topological-sort
  • DFS-based topological sort
  • topological_sort.py
  • TopologicalSort.java
  • NumberOfProvinces.java (Connected Components / Union Find)
  • MinimumHeightTrees.java (Tree Centroid Finding)

Problem Categories

1. Course Scheduling

Problems involving prerequisite relationships and course ordering.

  • Pattern: Build dependency graph, check for cycles, find valid ordering
  • Key Problems: LC 207, 210, 630, 1462

2. Task Scheduling

Problems involving task dependencies and parallel execution.

  • Pattern: Find minimum time, parallel processing levels
  • Key Problems: LC 1136, 2050, 1857

3. Lexicographical Ordering

Problems requiring smallest/largest lexicographical topological order.

  • Pattern: Priority queue for ordering, alien dictionary
  • Key Problems: LC 269, 953, 1203

4. Build Order & Dependencies

Problems involving build systems and package dependencies.

  • Pattern: Detect cycles, find build order, handle groups
  • Key Problems: LC 444, 802, 851

5. Graph Layering

Problems involving level-by-level processing in DAGs.

  • Pattern: BFS with levels, longest path in DAG
  • Key Problems: LC 2192, 2115, 1857

6. Cycle Detection & Safe States

Problems focused on detecting cycles and finding safe nodes.

  • Pattern: Three-color DFS, safe states identification
  • Key Problems: LC 802, 207, 1059

7. Connected Components (Union Find / DFS)

Problems involving finding connected components in undirected graphs.

  • Pattern: Union Find with path compression, DFS/BFS traversal to count components
  • Key Problems: LC 547, 200, 323, 684

8. Tree Centroid Finding

Problems involving finding the center/centroid of undirected trees.

  • Pattern: Leaf trimming layer by layer, similar to topological sort for undirected trees
  • Key Problems: LC 310, Tree diameter, Tree center

Core Templates

Template 1: BFS (Kahn’s Algorithm)

1def topologicalSort_BFS(numNodes, edges):
2    """
3    BFS-based topological sort using in-degree tracking.
4    Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V)
5    """
6    from collections import defaultdict, deque
7    
8    # Build graph and calculate in-degrees
9    graph = defaultdict(list)
10    in_degree = [0] * numNodes
11    
12    for u, v in edges:
13        graph[u].append(v)
14        in_degree[v] += 1
15    
16    # Initialize queue with nodes having no dependencies
17    queue = deque([i for i in range(numNodes) if in_degree[i] == 0])
18    result = []
19    
20    while queue:
21        node = queue.popleft()
22        result.append(node)
23        
24        # Process neighbors
25        for neighbor in graph[node]:
26            in_degree[neighbor] -= 1
27            if in_degree[neighbor] == 0:
28                queue.append(neighbor)
29    
30    # Check for cycles
31    return result if len(result) == numNodes else []
32
33# Java version
34public List<Integer> topologicalSort_BFS(int numNodes, int[][] edges) {
35    Map<Integer, List<Integer>> graph = new HashMap<>();
36    int[] inDegree = new int[numNodes];
37    
38    // Build graph
39    for (int i = 0; i < numNodes; i++) {
40        graph.put(i, new ArrayList<>());
41    }
42    
43    for (int[] edge : edges) {
44        graph.get(edge[0]).add(edge[1]);
45        inDegree[edge[1]]++;
46    }
47    
48    // BFS
49    Queue<Integer> queue = new LinkedList<>();
50    for (int i = 0; i < numNodes; i++) {
51        if (inDegree[i] == 0) queue.offer(i);
52    }
53    
54    List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
55    while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
56        int node = queue.poll();
57        result.add(node);
58        
59        for (int neighbor : graph.get(node)) {
60            if (--inDegree[neighbor] == 0) {
61                queue.offer(neighbor);
62            }
63        }
64    }
65    
66    return result.size() == numNodes ? result : new ArrayList<>();
67}

Template 2: DFS (Three-Color)

1def topologicalSort_DFS(numNodes, edges):
2    """
3    DFS-based topological sort with three-color marking.
4    Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V)
5    """
6    from collections import defaultdict
7    
8    graph = defaultdict(list)
9    for u, v in edges:
10        graph[u].append(v)
11    
12    # 0: white (unvisited), 1: gray (visiting), 2: black (visited)
13    color = [0] * numNodes
14    result = []
15    has_cycle = False
16    
17    def dfs(node):
18        nonlocal has_cycle
19        if color[node] == 1:  # Gray = cycle detected
20            has_cycle = True
21            return
22        if color[node] == 2:  # Black = already processed
23            return
24        
25        color[node] = 1  # Mark as visiting
26        for neighbor in graph[node]:
27            dfs(neighbor)
28        color[node] = 2  # Mark as visited
29        result.append(node)  # Add to result in reverse order
30    
31    for i in range(numNodes):
32        if color[i] == 0:
33            dfs(i)
34    
35    return [] if has_cycle else result[::-1]
36
37# Java version
38public List<Integer> topologicalSort_DFS(int numNodes, int[][] edges) {
39    Map<Integer, List<Integer>> graph = new HashMap<>();
40    for (int i = 0; i < numNodes; i++) {
41        graph.put(i, new ArrayList<>());
42    }
43    for (int[] edge : edges) {
44        graph.get(edge[0]).add(edge[1]);
45    }
46    
47    int[] color = new int[numNodes]; // 0: white, 1: gray, 2: black
48    List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
49    boolean[] hasCycle = {false};
50    
51    for (int i = 0; i < numNodes; i++) {
52        if (color[i] == 0) {
53            dfs(i, graph, color, result, hasCycle);
54        }
55    }
56    
57    if (hasCycle[0]) return new ArrayList<>();
58    Collections.reverse(result);
59    return result;
60}
61
62private void dfs(int node, Map<Integer, List<Integer>> graph, 
63                 int[] color, List<Integer> result, boolean[] hasCycle) {
64    if (color[node] == 1) {
65        hasCycle[0] = true;
66        return;
67    }
68    if (color[node] == 2) return;
69    
70    color[node] = 1;
71    for (int neighbor : graph.get(node)) {
72        dfs(neighbor, graph, color, result, hasCycle);
73    }
74    color[node] = 2;
75    result.add(node);
76}

Template 3: DFS (Stack-based)

1# V0
2# IDEA : implement topologicalSortUtil, topologicalSort, and addEdge methods
3# step 1) maintain a stack, save "ordering" nodes in it (and return in final step)
4# step 2) init visited as [False]*self.V  (all nodes are NOT visited yet)
5# step 3) iterate over all vertices in graph, if not visited, then run topologicalSortUtil
6# step 4) return result (stack)
7from collections import defaultdict
8class Graph:
9    def __init__(self, vertices):
10        self.graph = defaultdict(list)
11        self.V = vertices
12
13    # for build graph
14    def addEdge(self, u, v):
15        self.graph[u].append(v)
16
17    def topologicalSortUtil(self, v, visited, stack):
18        visited[v] = True
19
20        ### NOTE this !!! (self.graph[v])
21        for k in self.graph[v]:
22            if visited[k] == False:
23                self.topologicalSortUtil(k, visited, stack)
24        # stack.insert(0,v) # instead of insert v to idx = 0, we can still append v to stack and reverse it and return (e.g. return stack[::-1])
25        """
26        ### NOTE !! stack.append(v) is wrong, we SHOULD use  stack.insert(0,v)
27        """
28        stack.insert(0,v)
29
30    def topologicalSort(self):
31        visited = [False] * self.V
32        stack = []
33        ### NOTE this !!! (range(self.V))
34        for v in range(self.V):
35            # call tologicalSortUtil only if visited[v] == False (the vertice is not visited yet)
36            if visited[v] == False:
37                self.topologicalSortUtil(v, visited, stack)
38        # return the result in inverse order
39        return stack[::-1]
40
41### TEST
42{"A": 0, "B":1, "C":2, "D": 3}
43v = 4
44g = Graph(v)
45g.addEdge(0, 1)
46g.addEdge(0, 2)
47g.addEdge(2, 3)
48g.addEdge(3, 1)
49
50print (g.graph)
51
52# ans should be TableB, TableD, TableC, TableA.
53r = g.topologicalSort()
54print (r)

Template 4: Lexicographical Order

1def topologicalSort_Lexicographical(numNodes, edges):
2    """
3    Find smallest lexicographical topological order.
4    Time: O((V + E) log V), Space: O(V)
5    """
6    from collections import defaultdict
7    import heapq
8    
9    graph = defaultdict(list)
10    in_degree = [0] * numNodes
11    
12    for u, v in edges:
13        graph[u].append(v)
14        in_degree[v] += 1
15    
16    # Use min-heap for smallest lexicographical order
17    heap = [i for i in range(numNodes) if in_degree[i] == 0]
18    heapq.heapify(heap)
19    result = []
20    
21    while heap:
22        node = heapq.heappop(heap)
23        result.append(node)
24        
25        for neighbor in graph[node]:
26            in_degree[neighbor] -= 1
27            if in_degree[neighbor] == 0:
28                heapq.heappush(heap, neighbor)
29    
30    return result if len(result) == numNodes else []
31
32# Java version
33public List<Integer> topologicalSort_Lexicographical(int numNodes, int[][] edges) {
34    Map<Integer, List<Integer>> graph = new HashMap<>();
35    int[] inDegree = new int[numNodes];
36    
37    for (int i = 0; i < numNodes; i++) {
38        graph.put(i, new ArrayList<>());
39    }
40    
41    for (int[] edge : edges) {
42        graph.get(edge[0]).add(edge[1]);
43        inDegree[edge[1]]++;
44    }
45    
46    // Min-heap for lexicographical order
47    PriorityQueue<Integer> pq = new PriorityQueue<>();
48    for (int i = 0; i < numNodes; i++) {
49        if (inDegree[i] == 0) pq.offer(i);
50    }
51    
52    List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
53    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
54        int node = pq.poll();
55        result.add(node);
56        
57        for (int neighbor : graph.get(node)) {
58            if (--inDegree[neighbor] == 0) {
59                pq.offer(neighbor);
60            }
61        }
62    }
63    
64    return result.size() == numNodes ? result : new ArrayList<>();
65}

Template 5: All Topological Orders

1def allTopologicalSorts(graph, in_degree, path, result, visited):
2    """
3    Find all possible topological orderings.
4    Time: O(V! × (V + E)), Space: O(V)
5    """
6    if len(path) == len(graph):
7        result.append(path[:])
8        return
9    
10    for node in range(len(graph)):
11        if in_degree[node] == 0 and not visited[node]:
12            # Choose node
13            visited[node] = True
14            path.append(node)
15            
16            # Update in-degrees
17            for neighbor in graph[node]:
18                in_degree[neighbor] -= 1
19            
20            # Recurse
21            allTopologicalSorts(graph, in_degree, path, result, visited)
22            
23            # Backtrack
24            for neighbor in graph[node]:
25                in_degree[neighbor] += 1
26            path.pop()
27            visited[node] = False
28
29# Usage
30def findAllOrders(numNodes, edges):
31    from collections import defaultdict
32    
33    graph = defaultdict(list)
34    in_degree = [0] * numNodes
35    
36    for u, v in edges:
37        graph[u].append(v)
38        in_degree[v] += 1
39    
40    result = []
41    visited = [False] * numNodes
42    allTopologicalSorts(graph, in_degree, [], result, visited)
43    return result

Template 6: Parallel Task Scheduling

1def parallelTaskScheduling(numTasks, edges, times):
2    """
3    Find minimum time to complete all tasks with parallel execution.
4    Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V)
5    """
6    from collections import defaultdict, deque
7    
8    graph = defaultdict(list)
9    in_degree = [0] * numTasks
10    
11    for u, v in edges:
12        graph[u].append(v)
13        in_degree[v] += 1
14    
15    # Track completion time for each task
16    completion_time = [0] * numTasks
17    queue = deque()
18    
19    # Initialize with tasks having no dependencies
20    for i in range(numTasks):
21        if in_degree[i] == 0:
22            queue.append(i)
23            completion_time[i] = times[i]
24    
25    while queue:
26        task = queue.popleft()
27        
28        for next_task in graph[task]:
29            # Update completion time
30            completion_time[next_task] = max(
31                completion_time[next_task],
32                completion_time[task] + times[next_task]
33            )
34            
35            in_degree[next_task] -= 1
36            if in_degree[next_task] == 0:
37                queue.append(next_task)
38    
39    return max(completion_time)
40
41# Java version
42public int parallelTaskScheduling(int numTasks, int[][] edges, int[] times) {
43    Map<Integer, List<Integer>> graph = new HashMap<>();
44    int[] inDegree = new int[numTasks];
45    int[] completionTime = new int[numTasks];
46    
47    for (int i = 0; i < numTasks; i++) {
48        graph.put(i, new ArrayList<>());
49    }
50    
51    for (int[] edge : edges) {
52        graph.get(edge[0]).add(edge[1]);
53        inDegree[edge[1]]++;
54    }
55    
56    Queue<Integer> queue = new LinkedList<>();
57    for (int i = 0; i < numTasks; i++) {
58        if (inDegree[i] == 0) {
59            queue.offer(i);
60            completionTime[i] = times[i];
61        }
62    }
63    
64    while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
65        int task = queue.poll();
66        
67        for (int nextTask : graph.get(task)) {
68            completionTime[nextTask] = Math.max(
69                completionTime[nextTask],
70                completionTime[task] + times[nextTask]
71            );
72            
73            if (--inDegree[nextTask] == 0) {
74                queue.offer(nextTask);
75            }
76        }
77    }
78    
79    int maxTime = 0;
80    for (int time : completionTime) {
81        maxTime = Math.max(maxTime, time);
82    }
83    return maxTime;
84}

Template 7: Tree Centroid Finding (Leaf Trimming for Undirected Trees)

1def findMinHeightTrees(n, edges):
2    """
3    Find tree centroids using leaf trimming (similar to Kahn's Algorithm for undirected trees).
4    Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V)
5
6    Key Insight:
7    - For undirected trees, leaves are nodes with degree = 1
8    - Remove leaves layer by layer until 1-2 nodes remain
9    - These remaining nodes are the centroids (MHT roots)
10    - Different from standard topological sort: works on undirected trees, not DAGs
11    """
12    from collections import deque
13
14    # Edge case: single node
15    if n == 1:
16        return [0]
17
18    # Build adjacency list and track degrees
19    graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]
20    degree = [0] * n
21
22    for u, v in edges:
23        graph[u].append(v)
24        graph[v].append(u)
25        degree[u] += 1
26        degree[v] += 1
27
28    # Initialize queue with all leaf nodes (degree = 1)
29    leaves = deque([i for i in range(n) if degree[i] == 1])
30
31    # Trim leaves layer by layer
32    remaining = n
33    while remaining > 2:
34        leaf_count = len(leaves)
35        remaining -= leaf_count
36
37        for _ in range(leaf_count):
38            leaf = leaves.popleft()
39
40            # Process neighbors of current leaf
41            for neighbor in graph[leaf]:
42                degree[neighbor] -= 1
43                # If neighbor becomes a leaf, add to queue
44                if degree[neighbor] == 1:
45                    leaves.append(neighbor)
46
47    # The remaining nodes (1 or 2) are the centroids
48    return list(leaves)
49
50# Java version
51public List<Integer> findMinHeightTrees(int n, int[][] edges) {
52    // Edge case: single node
53    if (n == 1) {
54        return Collections.singletonList(0);
55    }
56
57    // Build adjacency list and track degrees
58    List<List<Integer>> graph = new ArrayList<>();
59    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
60        graph.add(new ArrayList<>());
61    }
62
63    int[] degree = new int[n];
64
65    for (int[] edge : edges) {
66        int u = edge[0], v = edge[1];
67        graph.get(u).add(v);
68        graph.get(v).add(u);
69        degree[u]++;
70        degree[v]++;
71    }
72
73    // Initialize queue with all leaf nodes (degree = 1)
74    Queue<Integer> leaves = new LinkedList<>();
75    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
76        if (degree[i] == 1) {
77            leaves.offer(i);
78        }
79    }
80
81    // Trim leaves layer by layer
82    int remaining = n;
83    while (remaining > 2) {
84        int leafCount = leaves.size();
85        remaining -= leafCount;
86
87        for (int i = 0; i < leafCount; i++) {
88            int leaf = leaves.poll();
89
90            // Process neighbors of current leaf
91            for (int neighbor : graph.get(leaf)) {
92                degree[neighbor]--;
93                // If neighbor becomes a leaf, add to queue
94                if (degree[neighbor] == 1) {
95                    leaves.offer(neighbor);
96                }
97            }
98        }
99    }
100
101    // The remaining nodes (1 or 2) are the centroids
102    return new ArrayList<>(leaves);
103}

Template 8: Union Find (Connected Components)

1class UnionFind:
2    """
3    Union Find with path compression and union by rank.
4    Time: O(α(N)) per operation (nearly O(1)), Space: O(N)
5    """
6    def __init__(self, n):
7        self.parent = list(range(n))
8        self.rank = [0] * n
9        self.count = n  # number of connected components
10
11    def find(self, x):
12        if self.parent[x] != x:
13            self.parent[x] = self.find(self.parent[x])  # path compression
14        return self.parent[x]
15
16    def union(self, x, y):
17        px, py = self.find(x), self.find(y)
18        if px == py:
19            return
20        # union by rank
21        if self.rank[px] < self.rank[py]:
22            px, py = py, px
23        self.parent[py] = px
24        if self.rank[px] == self.rank[py]:
25            self.rank[px] += 1
26        self.count -= 1
27
28# Usage: LC 547 Number of Provinces
29def findCircleNum(isConnected):
30    n = len(isConnected)
31    uf = UnionFind(n)
32    for i in range(n):
33        for j in range(i + 1, n):
34            if isConnected[i][j] == 1:
35                uf.union(i, j)
36    return uf.count
37
38# Java version
39class UnionFind {
40    int[] parent;
41    int[] rank;
42    int count;
43
44    UnionFind(int n) {
45        parent = new int[n];
46        rank = new int[n];
47        count = n;
48        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
49            parent[i] = i;
50        }
51    }
52
53    int find(int x) {
54        if (parent[x] != x) {
55            parent[x] = find(parent[x]); // path compression
56        }
57        return parent[x];
58    }
59
60    void union(int x, int y) {
61        int px = find(x), py = find(y);
62        if (px == py) return;
63        // union by rank
64        if (rank[px] < rank[py]) {
65            parent[px] = py;
66        } else if (rank[px] > rank[py]) {
67            parent[py] = px;
68        } else {
69            parent[py] = px;
70            rank[px]++;
71        }
72        count--;
73    }
74}
75
76// Usage: LC 547 Number of Provinces
77public int findCircleNum(int[][] isConnected) {
78    int n = isConnected.length;
79    UnionFind uf = new UnionFind(n);
80    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
81        for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
82            if (isConnected[i][j] == 1) {
83                uf.union(i, j);
84            }
85        }
86    }
87    return uf.count;
88}

Problem Classification

Problem Difficulty Category Key Technique
207. Course Schedule Medium Course Scheduling Cycle Detection
210. Course Schedule II Medium Course Scheduling BFS/DFS
269. Alien Dictionary Hard Lexicographical Character Ordering
310. Minimum Height Trees Medium Tree Centroid Leaf Trimming
444. Sequence Reconstruction Medium Build Order Unique Ordering
630. Course Schedule III Hard Course Scheduling Greedy + Heap
802. Find Eventual Safe States Medium Cycle Detection Reverse Graph
851. Loud and Rich Medium Graph Layering DFS + Memoization
953. Verifying an Alien Dictionary Easy Lexicographical Order Validation
1059. All Paths from Source Lead to Destination Medium Cycle Detection DFS
1136. Parallel Courses Medium Task Scheduling Level BFS
1203. Sort Items by Groups Respecting Dependencies Hard Build Order Double Topological
1462. Course Schedule IV Medium Course Scheduling Transitive Closure
1857. Largest Color Value in a Directed Graph Hard Graph Layering DP on DAG
2050. Parallel Courses III Hard Task Scheduling Time Calculation
2115. Find All Possible Recipes from Given Supplies Medium Build Order Modified BFS
547. Number of Provinces Medium Connected Components Union Find / DFS
2192. All Ancestors of a Node in a Directed Acyclic Graph Medium Graph Layering DFS/BFS

All Paths Safe 1059 DFS with path tracking
Detect Any Cycle 207 Three-color DFS

Connected Components (Union Find / DFS)

Pattern Problems Key Insight
Count Components 547, 323 Union Find or DFS traversal
Detect Redundant Edge 684 Union Find cycle detection
Number of Islands 200 DFS/BFS on grid

Tree Centroid Finding

Pattern Problems Key Insight
Find Tree Centers 310 Leaf trimming layer by layer (multi-source BFS inward)
Minimum Height Trees 310 BFS from leaves, stop at 1-2 nodes
Tree Diameter Related 310, 1245 Centroid is at diameter midpoint
Leaf Pruning / Coin Collection 2603 Trim leaves to remove unnecessary nodes
Sum of Distances in Tree 834 Rerooting DP, related to centroid concept

Decision Framework

1START: Topological Sort Problem
2│
3├── Working with undirected tree?
4│   │
5│   ├── YES → Finding tree center/centroid?
6│   │   │
7│   │   ├── YES → Use Template 7 (Tree Centroid Finding)
8│   │   │
9│   │   └── NO → Continue
10│   │
11│   └── NO → Continue
12│
13├── Need all valid orderings?
14│   │
15│   ├── YES → Use Template 5 (Backtracking)
16│   │
17│   └── NO → Continue
18│
19├── Need lexicographical order?
20│   │
21│   ├── YES → Use Template 4 (Priority Queue)
22│   │
23│   └── NO → Continue
24│
25├── Need parallel execution time?
26│   │
27│   ├── YES → Use Template 6 (Level BFS)
28│   │
29│   └── NO → Continue
30│
31├── Need to count connected components?
32│   │
33│   ├── YES → Use Template 8 (Union Find)
34│   │
35│   └── NO → Continue
36│
37├── Need cycle detection only?
38│   │
39│   ├── YES → Use Template 2 (Three-Color DFS)
40│   │
41│   └── NO → Continue
42│
43└── DEFAULT → Use Template 1 (BFS Kahn's Algorithm)

2) LC Example

2-2) Course Schedule II

1// java
2// LC 210
3// ref: leetcode_java/src/main/java/LeetCodeJava/BFS/CourseSchedule2.java
4public class CourseSchedule2 {
5
6    // V0
7    // IDEA : BFS Kahn's Algorithm with followingMap (pre -> next map)
8    // NOTE: With followingMap (Kahn's), we DO NOT need to reverse.
9    //       The order is already correct (starts with 0-dependency courses).
10    /**
11     * time = O(V + E)
12     * space = O(V + E)
13     */
14    public int[] findOrder(int numCourses, int[][] prerequisites) {
15        if (numCourses == 0) {
16            return null;
17        }
18        if (numCourses == 1) {
19            return new int[] { 0 };
20        }
21
22        /**
23         *  map : {pre_course : [next_course_1, next_course_2, ...] }
24         *  key is pre_course, value is the list of next-courses
25         */
26        Map<Integer, List<Integer>> followingMap = new HashMap<>();
27        int[] degrees = new int[numCourses];
28
29        for (int[] p : prerequisites) {
30            int cur = p[0];   // next course (depends on prev)
31            int prev = p[1];  // prerequisite course
32
33            // Build graph: prev -> [next courses]
34            if (!followingMap.containsKey(prev)) {
35                followingMap.put(prev, new ArrayList<>());
36            }
37            followingMap.get(prev).add(cur);
38
39            // Update in-degree for the next course
40            degrees[cur] += 1;
41        }
42
43        Queue<Integer> q = new LinkedList<>();
44        List<Integer> collected = new ArrayList<>();
45
46        // Add all courses with 0 in-degree to queue
47        for (int i = 0; i < degrees.length; i++) {
48            if (degrees[i] == 0) {
49                q.add(i);
50            }
51        }
52
53        while (!q.isEmpty()) {
54            int cur = q.poll();
55            collected.add(cur); // Add to result right after pop
56
57            if (followingMap.containsKey(cur)) {
58                for (int next : followingMap.get(cur)) {
59                    degrees[next] -= 1;
60                    if (degrees[next] == 0) {
61                        q.add(next);
62                    }
63                }
64            }
65        }
66
67        // Cycle check: if not all courses collected, cycle exists
68        if (collected.size() != numCourses) {
69            return new int[]{};
70        }
71
72        // NO need to reverse with followingMap approach
73        int[] res = new int[collected.size()];
74        for (int i = 0; i < collected.size(); i++) {
75            res[i] = collected.get(i);
76        }
77        return res;
78    }
79
80    // V1
81    // IDEA: DFS Cycle Detection
82    /**
83     * time = O(V + E)
84     * space = O(V + E)
85     */
86    public int[] findOrder_1_1(int numCourses, int[][] prerequisites) {
87        Map<Integer, List<Integer>> prereq = new HashMap<>();
88        for (int[] pair : prerequisites) {
89            prereq.computeIfAbsent(pair[0],
90                    k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(pair[1]);
91        }
92
93        List<Integer> output = new ArrayList<>();
94        Set<Integer> visit = new HashSet<>();
95        Set<Integer> cycle = new HashSet<>();
96
97        for (int course = 0; course < numCourses; course++) {
98            if (!dfs(course, prereq, visit, cycle, output)) {
99                return new int[0];
100            }
101        }
102
103        int[] result = new int[numCourses];
104        for (int i = 0; i < numCourses; i++) {
105            result[i] = output.get(i);
106        }
107        return result;
108    }
109
110    private boolean dfs(int course, Map<Integer, List<Integer>> prereq,
111                        Set<Integer> visit, Set<Integer> cycle,
112                        List<Integer> output) {
113        if (cycle.contains(course)) return false;  // cycle detected
114        if (visit.contains(course)) return true;    // already processed
115
116        cycle.add(course);
117        for (int pre : prereq.getOrDefault(course, Collections.emptyList())) {
118            if (!dfs(pre, prereq, visit, cycle, output)) {
119                return false;
120            }
121        }
122        cycle.remove(course);
123        visit.add(course);
124        output.add(course);
125        return true;
126    }
127}

1# LC 210 Course Schedule II
2# V0
3# IDEA : DFS + topological sort
4# SAME dfs logic as LC 207 (Course Schedule)
5from collections import defaultdict
6class Solution(object):
7    def findOrder(self, numCourses, prerequisites):
8        # edge case
9        if not prerequisites:
10            return [x for x in range(numCourses)]
11        
12        # help func : dfs
13        # 3 cases :  0 : unknown, 1 :visiting, 2 : visited   
14        def dfs(idx, visited, g, res):
15            if visited[idx] == 1:
16                return False
17            # NOTE !!! if visited[idx] == 2, means already visited, return True directly (and check next idx in range(numCourses))
18            if visited[idx] == 2:
19                return True
20            visited[idx] = 1
21            """
22            NOTE this !!!
23
24                1) for j in g[idx] (but not for i in range(numCourses))
25                2) go through idx in g[idx]
26            """
27            for j in g[idx]:
28                if not dfs(j, visited, g, res):
29                    return False
30            """
31            don't forget to make idx as visited (visited[idx] = 2)
32            """
33            visited[idx] = 2
34            """
35            NOTE : the main difference between LC 207, 210
36
37            -> we append idx to res (our ans)
38            """
39            res.append(idx)
40            return True
41        # init
42        visited = [0] * numCourses
43        # build grath
44        g = defaultdict(list)
45        for p in prerequisites:
46            g[p[0]].append(p[1])
47        res = []
48        """
49        NOTE :  go through idx in numCourses (for idx in range(numCourses))
50        """
51        for idx in range(numCourses):
52            if not dfs(idx, visited, g, res):
53                return []
54        return res
55
56# V0'
57# IDEA : DFS + topological sort
58# SAME dfs logic as LC 207 (Course Schedule)
59import collections
60class Solution:
61    def findOrder(self, numCourses, prerequisites):
62        # build graph
63        _graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
64        for i in range(len(prerequisites)):
65            _graph[prerequisites[i][0]].append(prerequisites[i][1])
66
67        visited = [0] * numCourses
68        res = []
69        for i in range(numCourses):
70            if not self.dfs(_graph, visited, i, res):
71                return []
72        print ("res = " + str(res))
73        return res
74
75    # 0 : unknown, 1 :visiting, 2 : visited    
76    def dfs(self, _graph, visited, i, res):
77        if visited[i] == 1:
78            return False
79        if visited[i] == 2:
80            return True
81        visited[i] = 1
82        for item in _graph[i]:
83            if not self.dfs(_graph, visited, item, res):
84                return False
85        visited[i] = 2
86        res.append(i)
87        return True
88
89# V0'
90# IDEA : DFS + topological sort
91# SAME dfs logic as LC 207 (Course Schedule)
92class Solution(object):
93    def findOrder(self, numCourses, prerequisites):
94        """
95        :type numCourses: int
96        :type prerequisites: List[List[int]]
97        :rtype: List[int]
98        """
99        graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
100        for u, v in prerequisites:
101            graph[u].append(v)
102        # 0 = Unknown, 1 = visiting, 2 = visited
103        visited = [0] * numCourses
104        path = []
105        for i in range(numCourses):
106            ### NOTE : if not a valid "prerequisites", then will return NULL list
107            if not self.dfs(graph, visited, i, path):
108                return []
109        return path
110    
111    def dfs(self, graph, visited, i, path):
112        # 0 = Unknown, 1 = visiting, 2 = visited
113        if visited[i] == 1: return False
114        if visited[i] == 2: return True
115        visited[i] = 1
116        for j in graph[i]:
117            if not self.dfs(graph, visited, j, path):
118                ### NOTE : the quit condition
119                return False
120        visited[i] = 2
121        path.append(i)
122        return True

2-2) Course Schedule

1// java
2// LC 207
3// same as LC 210

1# LC 207 Course Schedule
2# NOTE : there are also bracktrack, dfs approachs for this problem
3# V0
4# IDEA : LC Course Schedule II 
5from collections import defaultdict
6class Solution(object):
7    def canFinish(self, numCourses, prerequisites):
8        # edge case
9        if not prerequisites:
10            return [x for x in range(numCourses)]
11        
12        # help func : dfs
13        # 3 cases :  0 : unknown, 1 :visiting, 2 : visited   
14        def dfs(idx, visited, g, res):
15            if visited[idx] == 1:
16                return False
17            # NOTE !!! if visited[idx] == 2, means already visited, return True directly (and check next idx in range(numCourses))
18            if visited[idx] == 2:
19                return True
20            visited[idx] = 1
21            """
22            NOTE this !!!
23                1) for j in g[idx] (but not for i in range(numCourses))
24                2) go through idx in g[idx]
25            """
26            for j in g[idx]:
27                if not dfs(j, visited, g, res):
28                    return False
29            """
30            don't forget to make idx as visited (visited[idx] = 2)
31            """
32            visited[idx] = 2
33            """
34            NOTE : the main difference between LC 207, 210
35            -> we append idx to res (our ans)
36            """
37            res.append(idx)
38            return True
39        # init
40        visited = [0] * numCourses
41        # build grath
42        g = defaultdict(list)
43        for p in prerequisites:
44            g[p[0]].append(p[1])
45        res = []
46        """
47        NOTE :  go through idx in numCourses (for idx in range(numCourses))
48        """
49        for idx in range(numCourses):
50            if not dfs(idx, visited, g, res):
51                return False #[]
52        return len(res) > 0
53
54# V1
55# IDEA : Topological Sort
56# https://leetcode.com/problems/course-schedule/solution/
57class GNode(object):
58    """  data structure represent a vertex in the graph."""
59    def __init__(self):
60        self.inDegrees = 0
61        self.outNodes = []
62
63class Solution(object):
64    def canFinish(self, numCourses, prerequisites):
65        """
66        :type numCourses: int
67        :type prerequisites: List[List[int]]
68        :rtype: bool
69        """
70        from collections import defaultdict, deque
71        # key: index of node; value: GNode
72        graph = defaultdict(GNode)
73
74        totalDeps = 0
75        for relation in prerequisites:
76            nextCourse, prevCourse = relation[0], relation[1]
77            graph[prevCourse].outNodes.append(nextCourse)
78            graph[nextCourse].inDegrees += 1
79            totalDeps += 1
80
81        # we start from courses that have no prerequisites.
82        # we could use either set, stack or queue to keep track of courses with no dependence.
83        nodepCourses = deque()
84        for index, node in graph.items():
85            if node.inDegrees == 0:
86                nodepCourses.append(index)
87
88        removedEdges = 0
89        while nodepCourses:
90            # pop out course without dependency
91            course = nodepCourses.pop()
92
93            # remove its outgoing edges one by one
94            for nextCourse in graph[course].outNodes:
95                graph[nextCourse].inDegrees -= 1
96                removedEdges += 1
97                # while removing edges, we might discover new courses with prerequisites removed, i.e. new courses without prerequisites.
98                if graph[nextCourse].inDegrees == 0:
99                    nodepCourses.append(nextCourse)
100
101        if removedEdges == totalDeps:
102            return True
103        else:
104            # if there are still some edges left, then there exist some cycles
105            # Due to the dead-lock (dependencies), we cannot remove the cyclic edges
106            return False
107
108# V0
109# IDEA : DFS + topological sort 
110# dfs
111from collections import defaultdict
112class Solution(object):
113    def canFinish(self, numCourses, prerequisites):
114        # edge case
115        if not prerequisites:
116            return True
117        
118        # help func : dfs
119        # 3 cases :  0 : unknown, 1 :visiting, 2 : visited   
120        def dfs(idx, visited, g):
121            if visited[idx] == 1:
122                return False
123            # NOTE !!! if visited[idx] == 2, means already visited, return True directly (and check next idx in range(numCourses))
124            if visited[idx] == 2:
125                return True
126            visited[idx] = 1
127            """
128            NOTE this !!!
129
130                1) for j in g[idx] (but not for i in range(numCourses))
131                2) go through idx in g[idx]
132            """
133            for j in g[idx]:
134                if not dfs(j, visited, g):
135                    return False
136            """
137            don't forget to make idx as visited (visited[idx] = 2)
138            """
139            visited[idx] = 2
140            return True
141        # init
142        visited = [0] * numCourses
143        # build grath
144        g = defaultdict(list)
145        for p in prerequisites:
146            g[p[0]].append(p[1])
147        #print ("g = " + str(p))
148        # dfs
149        """
150        NOTE :  go through idx in numCourses (for idx in range(numCourses))
151        """
152        for idx in range(numCourses):
153            if not dfs(idx, visited, g):
154                return False
155        return True
156
157# V0
158# IDEA : DFS + topological sort 
159import collections
160class Solution:
161    def canFinish(self, numCourses, prerequisites):
162        _graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
163        for i in range(len(prerequisites)):
164            _graph[prerequisites[i][0]].append(prerequisites[i][1])
165
166        visited = [0] * numCourses
167        for i in range(numCourses):
168            if not self.dfs(_graph, visited, i):
169                return False
170        return True
171
172    # 0 : unknown, 1 :visiting, 2 : visited    
173    def dfs(self, _graph, visited, i):
174        if visited[i] == 1:
175            return False
176        if visited[i] == 2:
177            return True
178        visited[i] = 1
179        for item in _graph[i]:
180            if not self.dfs(_graph, visited, item):
181                return False
182        visited[i] = 2
183        return True
184
185# V0'
186# IDEA : BFS + topological sort 
187from collections import defaultdict, deque
188class Solution:
189    def canFinish(self, numCourses, prerequisites):
190        degree = defaultdict(int)   
191        graph = defaultdict(set)
192        q = deque()
193        
194        # init the courses with 0 deg
195        for i in range(numCourses):
196            degree[i] = 0
197        
198        # add 1 to degree of course that needs prereq
199        # build edge from prerequisite to child course (directed graph)
200        for pair in prerequisites:
201            degree[pair[0]] += 1
202            graph[pair[1]].add(pair[0])
203        
204        # start bfs queue with all classes that dont have a prerequisite
205        for key, val in degree.items():
206            if val == 0:
207                q.append(key)
208                
209        stack = []
210        
211        while q:
212            curr = q.popleft()
213            stack.append(curr)
214            for child in graph[curr]:
215                degree[child] -= 1
216                if degree[child] == 0:
217                    q.append(child)
218        
219        return len(stack) == numCourses

2-3) Number of Provinces (LC 547)

1// java
2// LC 547
3// ref: leetcode_java/src/main/java/LeetCodeJava/DFS/NumberOfProvinces.java
4
5public class NumberOfProvinces {
6
7    // V0
8    // IDEA: UNION FIND
9    /**
10     * time = O(N^2 * α(N))
11     * space = O(N)
12     */
13    public int findCircleNum(int[][] isConnected) {
14        int n = isConnected.length;
15        UnionFind uf = new UnionFind(n);
16
17        for (int y = 0; y < n; y++) {
18            for (int x = y + 1; x < n; x++) {
19                if (isConnected[y][x] == 1) {
20                    uf.union(y, x);
21                }
22            }
23        }
24
25        return uf.cluster;
26    }
27
28    class UnionFind {
29        int[] parents;
30        int cluster;
31
32        UnionFind(int n) {
33            this.parents = new int[n];
34            this.cluster = n;
35            for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
36                this.parents[i] = i;
37            }
38        }
39
40        public void union(int x, int y) {
41            int parentX = find(x);
42            int parentY = find(y);
43            if (parentX != parentY) {
44                this.parents[parentX] = parentY;
45                this.cluster -= 1;
46            }
47        }
48
49        public int find(int x) {
50            if (this.parents[x] != x) {
51                this.parents[x] = find(this.parents[x]); // path compression
52            }
53            return this.parents[x];
54        }
55    }
56
57    // V1
58    // IDEA: DFS directly on adjacency matrix
59    /**
60     * time = O(N^2)
61     * space = O(N)
62     */
63    public int findCircleNum_dfs(int[][] isConnected) {
64        int n = isConnected.length;
65        boolean[] visited = new boolean[n];
66        int provinces = 0;
67
68        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
69            if (!visited[i]) {
70                dfs(isConnected, visited, i, n);
71                provinces++;
72            }
73        }
74
75        return provinces;
76    }
77
78    private void dfs(int[][] isConnected, boolean[] visited, int city, int n) {
79        visited[city] = true;
80        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
81            if (isConnected[city][j] == 1 && !visited[j]) {
82                dfs(isConnected, visited, j, n);
83            }
84        }
85    }
86
87    // V2
88    // IDEA: BFS
89    /**
90     * time = O(N^2)
91     * space = O(N)
92     */
93    public int findCircleNum_bfs(int[][] isConnected) {
94        int n = isConnected.length;
95        boolean[] visited = new boolean[n];
96        int provinces = 0;
97
98        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
99            if (!visited[i]) {
100                provinces++;
101                Queue<Integer> q = new LinkedList<>();
102                q.offer(i);
103                visited[i] = true;
104
105                while (!q.isEmpty()) {
106                    int node = q.poll();
107                    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
108                        if (isConnected[node][j] == 1 && !visited[j]) {
109                            q.offer(j);
110                            visited[j] = true;
111                        }
112                    }
113                }
114            }
115        }
116
117        return provinces;
118    }
119}

1# python
2# LC 547 Number of Provinces
3# V0 - Union Find
4class UnionFind:
5    def __init__(self, n):
6        self.parent = list(range(n))
7        self.count = n
8
9    def find(self, x):
10        if self.parent[x] != x:
11            self.parent[x] = self.find(self.parent[x])
12        return self.parent[x]
13
14    def union(self, x, y):
15        px, py = self.find(x), self.find(y)
16        if px != py:
17            self.parent[px] = py
18            self.count -= 1
19
20class Solution:
21    def findCircleNum(self, isConnected):
22        n = len(isConnected)
23        uf = UnionFind(n)
24        for i in range(n):
25            for j in range(i + 1, n):
26                if isConnected[i][j] == 1:
27                    uf.union(i, j)
28        return uf.count
29
30# V1 - DFS
31class Solution:
32    def findCircleNum(self, isConnected):
33        n = len(isConnected)
34        visited = [False] * n
35        provinces = 0
36
37        def dfs(city):
38            visited[city] = True
39            for j in range(n):
40                if isConnected[city][j] == 1 and not visited[j]:
41                    dfs(j)
42
43        for i in range(n):
44            if not visited[i]:
45                dfs(i)
46                provinces += 1
47        return provinces

2-5) Alien Dictionary

1// java
2// LC 269
3
4 // V0
5    // IDEA: TOPOLOGICAL SORT (neetcode, comments created by gpt)
6    // TOPOLOGICAL SORT : `degrees`, map, BFS
7    public String foreignDictionary(String[] words) {
8        Map<Character, Set<Character>> adj = new HashMap<>();
9        // NOTE !!! we use `map` as degrees storage
10        Map<Character, Integer> indegree = new HashMap<>();
11
12        for (String word : words) {
13            for (char c : word.toCharArray()) {
14                adj.putIfAbsent(c, new HashSet<>());
15                indegree.putIfAbsent(c, 0);
16            }
17        }
18
19        /**
20         *   NOTE !!! below
21         *
22         *   -> build the character `ordering`
23         *
24         *  Loop Over Adjacent Word Pairs
25         *
26         *
27         *
28         * for (int i = 0; i < words.length - 1; i++) {
29         *     String w1 = words[i];
30         *     String w2 = words[i + 1];
31         *
32         * We are comparing each pair of consecutive
33         * words in the list (words[i] and words[i+1]).
34         *
35         * This is important because the alien language is
36         * sorted — and order relationships only exist between adjacent words.
37         *
38         */
39        for (int i = 0; i < words.length - 1; i++) {
40            String w1 = words[i];
41            String w2 = words[i + 1];
42
43            /**
44             *  NOTE !!! below
45             *
46             *
47             * int minLen = Math.min(w1.length(), w2.length());
48             * if (w1.length() > w2.length() &&
49             *     w1.substring(0, minLen).equals(w2.substring(0, minLen))) {
50             *     return "";
51             * }
52             *
53             *
54             * ->  This checks for a prefix violation:
55             * If w1 is longer than w2, and w2 is a prefix of w1, that’s `invalid`.
56             *
57             * Example:
58             *
59             *   words = ["apple", "app"]
60             *
61             *
62             * Here, app comes after apple,
63             * which is wrong because in a lexicographically sorted language,
64             * a shorter prefix should come before the longer word.
65             *
66             * -> Hence, we return "" to signal an invalid dictionary order.
67             *
68             */
69            int minLen = Math.min(w1.length(), w2.length());
70            // handle `ordering` edge case
71            // e.g. words = ["apple", "app"]
72            if (w1.length() > w2.length() &&
73                    w1.substring(0, minLen).equals(w2.substring(0, minLen))) {
74                return "";
75            }
76
77            /**
78             *  NOTE !!! below
79             *
80             *
81             *  This loop compares characters at each position j in w1 and w2.
82             *  The first place where they differ defines the ordering.
83             *
84             *
85             *  Example :
86             *
87             *    w1 = "wrt"
88             *    w2 = "wrf"
89             *
90             *
91             *
92             *  At index 2, 't' and 'f' differ → so we know:
93             * 't' < 'f' → Add a directed edge: t → f
94             *
95             * adj.get(w1.charAt(j)).add(w2.charAt(j)): Adds this edge in the adjacency list.
96             *
97             * indegree.put(...): Increments in-degree of the target node.
98             *
99             *
100             * NOTE !!!
101             *
102             * -> Then we break — we don’t look at further characters
103             *     -> because they don’t affect the order.
104             *
105             *
106             */
107            // compare the `first different character within w1, w2`
108            // The first place where they differ defines the ordering.
109            for (int j = 0; j < minLen; j++) {
110                if (w1.charAt(j) != w2.charAt(j)) {
111                    if (!adj.get(w1.charAt(j)).contains(w2.charAt(j))) {
112                        adj.get(w1.charAt(j)).add(w2.charAt(j));
113                        indegree.put(w2.charAt(j),
114                                indegree.get(w2.charAt(j)) + 1);
115                    }
116                    break;
117                }
118            }
119        }
120
121        Queue<Character> q = new LinkedList<>();
122        for (char c : indegree.keySet()) {
123            if (indegree.get(c) == 0) {
124                q.offer(c);
125            }
126        }
127
128        StringBuilder res = new StringBuilder();
129
130        while (!q.isEmpty()) {
131            char char_ = q.poll();
132            res.append(char_);
133            for (char neighbor : adj.get(char_)) {
134                indegree.put(neighbor, indegree.get(neighbor) - 1);
135                if (indegree.get(neighbor) == 0) {
136                    q.offer(neighbor);
137                }
138            }
139        }
140
141        if (res.length() != indegree.size()) {
142            return "";
143        }
144
145        return res.toString();
146    }
147

1# python
2# 269 alien-dictionary
3class Solution(object):
4    def alienOrder(self, words):
5        """
6        :type words: List[str]
7        :rtype: str
8        """
9        result, zero_in_degree_queue, in_degree, out_degree = [], collections.deque(), {}, {}
10        nodes = sets.Set()
11        for word in words:
12            for c in word:
13                nodes.add(c)
14         
15        for i in range(1, len(words)):
16            if len(words[i-1]) > len(words[i]) and \
17                words[i-1][:len(words[i])] == words[i]:
18                    return ""
19            self.findEdges(words[i - 1], words[i], in_degree, out_degree)
20         
21        for node in nodes:
22            if node not in in_degree:
23                zero_in_degree_queue.append(node)
24         
25        while zero_in_degree_queue:
26            precedence = zero_in_degree_queue.popleft()
27            result.append(precedence)
28             
29            if precedence in out_degree:
30                for c in out_degree[precedence]:
31                    in_degree[c].discard(precedence)
32                    if not in_degree[c]:
33                        zero_in_degree_queue.append(c)
34             
35                del out_degree[precedence]
36         
37        if out_degree:
38            return ""
39 
40        return "".join(result)
41 
42 
43    # Construct the graph.
44    def findEdges(self, word1, word2, in_degree, out_degree):
45        str_len = min(len(word1), len(word2))
46        for i in range(str_len):
47            if word1[i] != word2[i]:
48                if word2[i] not in in_degree:
49                    in_degree[word2[i]] = sets.Set()
50                if word1[i] not in out_degree:
51                    out_degree[word1[i]] = sets.Set()
52                in_degree[word2[i]].add(word1[i])
53                out_degree[word1[i]].add(word2[i])
54                break　　

2-6) Sequence Reconstruction

1// java
2// LC 444
3    // V1
4    // https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FHY1q1h9gq0
5    // https://www.jiakaobo.com/leetcode/444.%20Sequence%20Reconstruction.html
6    Map<Integer, Set<Integer>> map;
7    Map<Integer, Integer> indegree;
8
9    public boolean sequenceReconstruction_1(int[] nums, List<List<Integer>> sequences) {
10        map = new HashMap<>();
11        indegree = new HashMap<>();
12
13        for(List<Integer> seq: sequences) {
14            if(seq.size() == 1) {
15                addNode(seq.get(0));
16            } else {
17                for(int i = 0; i < seq.size() - 1; i++) {
18                    addNode(seq.get(i));
19                    addNode(seq.get(i + 1));
20
21                    // 加入子节点, 子节点增加一个入度
22                    // [1,2] => 1 -> 2
23                    // 1: [2]
24                    int curr = seq.get(i);
25                    int next = seq.get(i + 1);
26                    if(map.get(curr).add(next)) {
27                        indegree.put(next, indegree.get(next) + 1);
28                    }
29                }
30            }
31        }
32
33        Queue<Integer> queue = new LinkedList<>();
34        for(int key : indegree.keySet()) {
35            if(indegree.get(key) == 0){
36                queue.offer(key);
37            }
38        }
39
40        int index = 0;
41        while(!queue.isEmpty()) {
42            // 如果只有唯一解, 那么queue的大小永远都是1
43            if(queue.size() != 1) return false;
44
45            int curr = queue.poll();
46            if(curr != nums[index++]) return false;
47
48            for(int next: map.get(curr)) {
49                indegree.put(next, indegree.get(next) - 1);
50                if(indegree.get(next) == 0) {
51                    queue.offer(next);
52                }
53            }
54        }
55
56        return index == nums.length;
57    }
58
59    private void addNode(int node) {
60        if(!map.containsKey(node)) {
61            map.put(node, new HashSet<>());
62            indegree.put(node, 0);
63        }
64    }

2-7) Parallel Courses

1# LC 1136
2def minimumSemesters(n, relations):
3    """
4    Find minimum number of semesters to take all courses.
5    Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V)
6    """
7    from collections import defaultdict, deque
8    
9    graph = defaultdict(list)
10    in_degree = [0] * (n + 1)
11    
12    for pre, next_course in relations:
13        graph[pre].append(next_course)
14        in_degree[next_course] += 1
15    
16    queue = deque([i for i in range(1, n + 1) if in_degree[i] == 0])
17    semesters = 0
18    studied = 0
19    
20    while queue:
21        # Process all courses in current semester
22        semesters += 1
23        next_queue = []
24        
25        for _ in range(len(queue)):
26            course = queue.popleft()
27            studied += 1
28            
29            for next_course in graph[course]:
30                in_degree[next_course] -= 1
31                if in_degree[next_course] == 0:
32                    next_queue.append(next_course)
33        
34        queue.extend(next_queue)
35    
36    return semesters if studied == n else -1

2-8) Find Eventual Safe States

1# LC 802
2def eventualSafeNodes(graph):
3    """
4    Find all safe nodes (nodes from which all paths lead to terminal).
5    Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V)
6    """
7    n = len(graph)
8    # Reverse the graph
9    reverse_graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]
10    out_degree = [0] * n
11    
12    for i in range(n):
13        for j in graph[i]:
14            reverse_graph[j].append(i)
15        out_degree[i] = len(graph[i])
16    
17    # Start with terminal nodes (out-degree = 0)
18    from collections import deque
19    queue = deque([i for i in range(n) if out_degree[i] == 0])
20    safe = []
21    
22    while queue:
23        node = queue.popleft()
24        safe.append(node)
25        
26        for prev in reverse_graph[node]:
27            out_degree[prev] -= 1
28            if out_degree[prev] == 0:
29                queue.append(prev)
30    
31    return sorted(safe)

2-9) Minimum Height Trees (LC 310)

1// java
2// LC 310
3// IDEA: Tree Centroid Finding - Leaf Trimming (Topological Sort for Undirected Trees)
4public class MinimumHeightTrees {
5
6    /**
7     * Core Concept: Tree Centroid Finding via Leaf Trimming
8     *
9     * This is a special application of topological sort to UNDIRECTED TREES:
10     *
11     * Key Differences from Standard Topological Sort:
12     * 1. Works on UNDIRECTED trees (not DAGs)
13     * 2. Uses degree (not in-degree) - count all edges
14     * 3. Leaves are nodes with degree = 1 (not in-degree = 0)
15     * 4. Goal: Find centroid(s), not linear ordering
16     * 5. Result: 1 or 2 nodes (tree centers), not all nodes
17     *
18     * Algorithm (Leaf Trimming):
19     * 1. Build adjacency list + track degrees for undirected edges
20     * 2. Put all leaves (degree = 1) into queue
21     * 3. Remove leaves layer by layer (like peeling an onion)
22     * 4. When neighbors' degree becomes 1, they become new leaves
23     * 5. Stop when ≤ 2 nodes remain - these are the centroids
24     *
25     * Why it works:
26     * - The centroid(s) of a tree minimize the maximum distance to any leaf
27     * - By removing outer layers, we converge to the center
28     * - A tree can have at most 2 centroids (if diameter is even: 2, if odd: 1)
29     *
30     * Time: O(N) - Each node and edge processed once
31     * Space: O(N) - Adjacency list and queue storage
32     */
33    public List<Integer> findMinHeightTrees(int n, int[][] edges) {
34        // Edge case: single node tree
35        if (n == 1) {
36            return Collections.singletonList(0);
37        }
38
39        // Step 1: Build adjacency list and track degrees
40        List<List<Integer>> graph = new ArrayList<>();
41        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
42            graph.add(new ArrayList<>());
43        }
44
45        int[] degree = new int[n];
46
47        // For undirected tree: add edges in both directions
48        for (int[] edge : edges) {
49            int u = edge[0], v = edge[1];
50            graph.get(u).add(v);
51            graph.get(v).add(u);
52            degree[u]++;
53            degree[v]++;
54        }
55
56        // Step 2: Initialize queue with all leaf nodes (degree = 1)
57        Queue<Integer> leaves = new LinkedList<>();
58        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
59            if (degree[i] == 1) {
60                leaves.offer(i);
61            }
62        }
63
64        // Step 3: Trim leaves layer by layer
65        int remaining = n;
66
67        // Continue until only 1 or 2 nodes remain
68        while (remaining > 2) {
69            int leafCount = leaves.size();
70            remaining -= leafCount;
71
72            // Process all leaves in current layer
73            for (int i = 0; i < leafCount; i++) {
74                int leaf = leaves.poll();
75
76                // Update degrees of neighbors
77                for (int neighbor : graph.get(leaf)) {
78                    degree[neighbor]--;
79
80                    // If neighbor becomes a leaf, add to queue for next layer
81                    if (degree[neighbor] == 1) {
82                        leaves.offer(neighbor);
83                    }
84                }
85            }
86        }
87
88        // Step 4: The remaining nodes (1 or 2) are the centroids
89        return new ArrayList<>(leaves);
90    }
91}

1# python
2# LC 310
3# V0 - Tree Centroid Finding (Leaf Trimming for Undirected Trees)
4from collections import deque
5
6class Solution:
7    def findMinHeightTrees(self, n: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]:
8        """
9        Find tree centroids using leaf trimming.
10
11        Key Pattern: Similar to Kahn's Algorithm but for undirected trees
12        - Use degree (not in-degree)
13        - Leaves are nodes with degree = 1
14        - Trim layers until 1-2 nodes remain
15
16        Time: O(N), Space: O(N)
17        """
18        # Edge case
19        if n == 1:
20            return [0]
21
22        # Build adjacency list and track degrees
23        graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]
24        degree = [0] * n
25
26        for u, v in edges:
27            graph[u].append(v)
28            graph[v].append(u)
29            degree[u] += 1
30            degree[v] += 1
31
32        # Initialize queue with all leaves (degree = 1)
33        leaves = deque([i for i in range(n) if degree[i] == 1])
34
35        # Trim leaves layer by layer
36        remaining = n
37        while remaining > 2:
38            leaf_count = len(leaves)
39            remaining -= leaf_count
40
41            for _ in range(leaf_count):
42                leaf = leaves.popleft()
43
44                # Decrease degree of neighbors
45                for neighbor in graph[leaf]:
46                    degree[neighbor] -= 1
47                    # If neighbor becomes leaf, add to queue
48                    if degree[neighbor] == 1:
49                        leaves.append(neighbor)
50
51        # Return remaining centroids (1 or 2 nodes)
52        return list(leaves)
53
54
55# V1 - Alternative: Using Set for adjacency (faster removal)
56class Solution:
57    def findMinHeightTrees(self, n: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]:
58        if n == 1:
59            return [0]
60
61        # Build graph with sets for O(1) removal
62        graph = [set() for _ in range(n)]
63
64        for u, v in edges:
65            graph[u].add(v)
66            graph[v].add(u)
67
68        # Find initial leaves
69        leaves = [i for i in range(n) if len(graph[i]) == 1]
70
71        # Trim leaves until 1-2 nodes remain
72        while n > 2:
73            n -= len(leaves)
74            new_leaves = []
75
76            for leaf in leaves:
77                # Get the only neighbor
78                neighbor = graph[leaf].pop()
79                # Remove leaf from neighbor's adjacency set
80                graph[neighbor].remove(leaf)
81
82                # If neighbor becomes leaf, add to next layer
83                if len(graph[neighbor]) == 1:
84                    new_leaves.append(neighbor)
85
86            leaves = new_leaves
87
88        return leaves

Core Logic Explanation:

  1. Why Leaf Trimming Works:

    • Tree centroids minimize the height when used as roots
    • By removing outer layers (leaves), we converge to the center
    • Like peeling an onion from outside to inside

  2. Key Differences from Standard Topological Sort:

    • Graph Type: Undirected tree vs Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
    • Degree Tracking: Total degree vs in-degree
    • Leaf Definition: degree = 1 vs in-degree = 0
    • Goal: Find center(s) vs Find linear ordering
    • Result: 1-2 nodes vs All nodes in order

  3. Why At Most 2 Centroids:

    • If tree diameter is even → 2 center nodes
    • If tree diameter is odd → 1 center node
    • These nodes minimize the maximum distance to any leaf

Core Idea — BFS / Layer Trimming (Onion Peeling):

  • Think of the tree as an onion. The MHT roots are in the innermost layer
  • Start from the outermost leaves (degree = 1), peel them off layer by layer
  • Each layer removal reveals new leaves (neighbors whose degree drops to 1)
  • Stop when ≤ 2 nodes remain — these are the centroids
  • This is multi-source BFS from leaves inward, NOT BFS from a single root
1[0,1,2,3,4]  (linear tree: 0-1-2-3-4)
2
3→ remove 0,4       (outer leaves)
4→ remove 1,3       (new leaves after trimming)
5→ left with [2] ✅  (centroid)

Pattern Recognition — When to Use Leaf Trimming:

Signal Example
Find center/centroid of a tree LC 310
Minimize max distance from root to any leaf LC 310
Undirected tree + “optimal root” LC 310, tree radius
Peel layers from outside inward LC 310, onion structure
NOT a DAG, NOT directed edges Use this instead of standard topo sort

Key Observations:

  • A tree has at most 2 centroids: diameter even → 2 centers, diameter odd → 1 center
  • Brute force (BFS from every node) is O(N²) — TLE for large inputs
  • Leaf trimming achieves O(N) by processing each node and edge exactly once
  • Can use Set<Integer> adjacency for O(1) removal, or int[] degree array (simpler, preferred)

Classic Similar LCs:

LC # Problem Connection
310 Minimum Height Trees Core leaf trimming problem
207 Course Schedule Kahn’s algo — same BFS + degree pattern on DAG
210 Course Schedule II Kahn’s algo — produces ordering
834 Sum of Distances in Tree Tree centroid / rerooting DP
1245 Tree Diameter Find diameter → centroid is at midpoint
2603 Collect Coins in a Tree Leaf trimming to remove unnecessary nodes
1377 Frog Position After T Seconds BFS on tree from root
863 All Nodes Distance K in Binary Tree BFS from a node in tree

Summary & Interview Tips

Common Pitfalls

  1. Forgetting Cycle Detection: Always check if result size equals number of nodes
  2. Wrong Edge Direction: Remember edges go from prerequisite to dependent
  3. Not Handling Disconnected Components: Process all unvisited nodes
  4. Incorrect In-degree Initialization: Ensure all nodes are included
  5. Missing Edge Cases: Empty graph, single node, self-loops
  6. Confusing Degree vs In-degree: For undirected trees use total degree, for DAGs use in-degree
  7. Wrong Stopping Condition: For tree centroids, stop at ≤2 nodes (not when queue is empty)

Key Insights

  1. BFS vs DFS: BFS is easier for finding one order, DFS for all orders
  2. In-degree Tracking: Nodes with 0 in-degree can be processed
  3. Three-Color DFS: White (unvisited), Gray (visiting), Black (visited)
  4. Reverse Graph: Useful for problems like safe states
  5. Level Processing: For parallel execution and minimum time
  6. Tree Centroid Finding: For undirected trees, use degree (not in-degree), trim leaves layer by layer until 1-2 nodes remain
  7. Undirected vs Directed: Undirected trees need bidirectional edges and degree tracking, while DAGs use in-degree

Interview Approach

  1. Clarify Requirements:

    • Is the graph guaranteed to be a DAG?
    • Do we need all orderings or just one?
    • Are there any ordering preferences?

  2. Choose Algorithm:

    • Default to BFS (Kahn’s) for simplicity
    • Use DFS for recursive problems
    • Use priority queue for lexicographical order

  3. Handle Edge Cases:

    • Empty graph
    • Single node
    • Disconnected components
    • Cycles in graph

  4. Optimize if Needed:

    • Early termination for cycle detection
    • Space optimization with in-place modifications
    • Time optimization with better data structures

Time/Space Complexity Summary

Operation Time Space Notes
Build Graph O(E) O(V + E) Adjacency list
Calculate In-degrees O(E) O(V) Array or map
BFS/DFS Traversal O(V + E) O(V) Visit each node/edge once
Cycle Detection O(V + E) O(V) Three-color marking
All Orderings O(V! × E) O(V) Exponential for all permutations

Related Concepts

  • Strongly Connected Components: Tarjan’s/Kosaraju’s algorithm
  • Shortest Path in DAG: Topological sort + relaxation
  • Critical Path Method: Project scheduling
  • Dependency Resolution: Package managers, build systems
  • Dataflow Analysis: Compiler optimization
